title: DeepMind Proposes Global AI Watchdog, Trump Tightens Defense Supply Chains, AMD Takes On Nvidia With Helios
subhead: Hassabis pushes for a FINRA-style AI regulator just as frontier model releases face government gatekeeping; a new executive order forces Pentagon contractors to map and wean off Chinese minerals; and AMD ships its first rack-scale AI system with Microsoft on board.

sections.heading: DeepMind Proposes a Global AI Watchdog
sections.body:
Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis published a proposal last week for an international watchdog body that would conduct 'rigorous' pre-release testing of frontier AI models, modeled on financial regulators like FINRA. The plan specifies independent technical experts as reviewers, funding from leading AI firms, and a 30-day review window before model launches — with the option to 'ratchet up' scrutiny, including coordinating an industry slowdown among leading labs.

Hassabis said he decided to go public after the controversy around Anthropic's Mythos model, which he called a 'warning shot' for society. He separately flagged biosecurity risks from increasingly capable models. The proposal was quickly endorsed by OpenAI's Sam Altman, who called it 'thoughtful,' and Elon Musk, who called it 'a good starting point.' Hassabis has meetings scheduled in Washington this week with U.S. policymakers.

The timing is pointed. Anthropic and OpenAI have both delayed wide releases of their latest models under pressure from the Trump administration, raising questions abroad about U.S. control over AI access. Hassabis acknowledged DeepMind has also been in government talks over its newest models, saying the current ad hoc approach 'isn't very sustainable in the longer term.' The challenge: the U.S., EU, and China are all moving in different regulatory directions, and Congress has yet to pass federal AI legislation. One unnamed AI leader noted that industry consensus alone won't be enough — the political hurdles of getting Washington on board remain formidable.
sections.whyItMatters: If adopted, this would be the first structured, international pre-deployment review mechanism for frontier AI — a step beyond the voluntary commitments labs have made so far. But it arrives as governments are pulling in opposite directions: the U.S. is gatekeeping model access, China just launched its own governance body (WAICO), and the EU is implementing its AI Act. The question is whether any single framework can bridge these divergent paths.

sections.heading: Trump Executive Order Tightens Defense Supply Chain Waivers
sections.body:
President Trump signed an executive order on Monday making it significantly harder for U.S. defense contractors to obtain waivers allowing them to buy critical minerals and other materials from China and other prohibited foreign suppliers. The order is the administration's most aggressive move yet to sever the Pentagon's dependence on overseas supply chains.

Under the new rules, contractors seeking a waiver must prove they searched for alternatives, explain where their materials come from, and lay out a plan to move away from prohibited suppliers. Companies that fail to demonstrate progress toward domestic sourcing face loss of contracts. The order also requires contractors to map critical defense supply chains from raw materials through finished products, extending government visibility beyond prime contractors to lower-tier suppliers, and to assess suppliers for foreign ownership, financial vulnerability, and manufacturing risks.

White House adviser Peter Navarro was blunt: 'No more: we tried nothing and we're out of options.' He framed the requirement as 'battlefield preparation,' arguing the Pentagon needs to know whether weapons systems depend on foreign-controlled suppliers before a conflict begins. The order comes as the Pentagon pushes contractors like Lockheed Martin and Boeing to rapidly expand weapons production while facing persistent vulnerabilities in the minerals and components that feed advanced systems.
sections.whyItMatters: This EO turns a voluntary compliance regime into a mandatory one with teeth. The Pentagon has known for years that its supply chains run through China for rare earths, specialty alloys, and electronic components — this order forces contractors to document those dependencies and present plans to eliminate them, or lose their contracts. The practical question is whether alternative domestic or allied sources exist at scale, or whether the order simply creates compliance friction without shortening the lines.

sections.heading: AMD Launches Helios, Its First Rack-Scale AI System
sections.body:
AMD on Monday formally launched Helios, its first rack-scale AI computing system, and announced Microsoft as its newest customer. The system brings together four things AMD does in-house — GPUs, CPUs, networking, and software — into a single rack with 18 compute trays, each carrying four Instinct GPUs powered by a single EPYC CPU. Meta, OpenAI, Oracle, and India's Tata Consultancy Services had already committed; Microsoft adds the most significant cloud operator yet.

AMD data center head Forrest Norrod said the company is focused on 'the lowest cost per token, all in.' The Futurum Group estimates Helios will cost between $5 million and $5.5 million per rack, compared with $3.5–4 million for Nvidia's second-generation Vera Rubin system. Helios is also wider and heavier. But AMD is targeting 20–25% data center GPU market share over time, up from roughly 4.5% today — a shift that, as Futurum CEO Daniel Newman noted, would represent 'hundreds of billions of dollars of revenue' given the market's scale.

CEO Lisa Su has previously said Helios offers 'significant benefits' over Nvidia's systems on inference and memory bandwidth. AMD says eight of the top ten AI companies now run workloads on its Instinct GPUs, including OpenAI, Cohere, and SpaceX's AI division. Shipments begin later this year.
sections.whyItMatters: Nvidia controls over 95% of the data center GPU market. Helios is the first rack-scale system that gives hyperscalers a credible second option — not a niche alternative, but a full-stack competitor with Microsoft, Meta, and OpenAI already on board. If AMD can deliver at scale and on cost, it reshapes the compute sovereignty calculus: countries and companies that have been entirely dependent on Nvidia now have a genuine alternative.

sections.heading: Kimi K3 Joins the Chinese AI Model Surge
sections.body:
Beijing-based startup Moonshot AI released its Kimi K3 model on Friday, and early benchmarks place it at or near the top of coding capability rankings. Arena, an AI evaluation platform, ranked K3 first in front-end coding capability among all large language models, and co-founder Anastasios Angelopoulos called it 'the single biggest release of the year,' marking a moment when open-source Chinese models are surpassing closed U.S. systems on some benchmarks.

The timing was deliberate: K3 launched just before Xi Jinping's opening address to WAIC in Shanghai. Moonshot, a Huawei partner, has not disclosed what hardware trained K3. Its pricing is the highest yet for a Chinese AI model but still roughly half the cost of OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol, according to Bank of America analysts. Demand was intense enough that Moonshot paused new signups within days of launch due to capacity constraints.

The release follows Zhipu's GLM-5.2 in June and continues a pattern where Chinese open-weight models are closing the gap with — and in some benchmarks surpassing — U.S. frontier models at lower price points. Tech analyst Patrick Moorhead called the market reaction an 'overreaction shockingly similar' to DeepSeek's January 2025 release. The distillation dispute also continues: Anthropic has accused Moonshot, DeepSeek, and MiniMax of illicitly extracting capabilities from Claude. K3's release adds fuel to that debate.
sections.whyItMatters: K3 is not just another model launch — it is the latest data point that Chinese AI labs can produce frontier-quality models despite chip export controls, and price them aggressively enough to erode the revenue model of U.S. providers. The capacity constraints (signup pause) suggest the compute bottleneck still bites, but the performance is real.

sections.heading: Russian Intelligence Hijacks IP Cameras Across NATO and Ukraine
sections.body:
A joint advisory from the Netherlands' AIVD and MIVD intelligence services, published July 10, details a systematic Russian intelligence operation that is hijacking internet-connected security cameras across Europe and Ukraine to monitor military transport routes, weapons shipments, and troop positions.

In Ukraine, camera access has been 'used in attempts to neutralise Ukrainian military personnel' and destroy equipment, the advisory states — turning exposed roadside and business cameras into targeting aids. Across EU and NATO states, the same access is collecting military intelligence unrelated to the Ukraine war.

The attack methodology is low-sophistication: operators scan for exposed devices running default passwords, obsolete firmware, and factory settings, then use image-recognition software to automatically flag military vehicles and cargo. Internet-scanning firm Censys identified over 87,000 cameras across NATO and Ukraine running services matching known-exploited vulnerabilities — with over 4,000 in Ukraine alone. In the Netherlands alone, Censys found 45,386 cameras reachable from the public internet, 1,992 running vulnerable services. The Dutch services said they had confirmed only a small number of actual breaches on military logistics routes, but warned the exposed surface is enormous.
sections.whyItMatters: This is a case where the vulnerability isn't a sophisticated zero-day or advanced persistent threat — it is default passwords and unpatched firmware. The lesson is that physical-digital convergence at the edge (cameras, IoT sensors, industrial devices) creates intelligence surfaces that adversaries can exploit at scale with minimal technical effort. Russian intelligence is proving that you don't need to breach a hardened network when you can watch the parking lot.

sections.heading: Huawei Unveils Atlas 950 SuperPoD — AI Compute Without U.S. Parts
sections.body:
At the World AI Conference in Shanghai, Huawei publicly displayed the Atlas 950 SuperPoD for the first time — a system linking up to 8,192 of its own Ascend 950DT processors into a single computing node. Huawei claims the full system delivers one exaflops at FP8 precision and two exaflops at FP4, with 256 terabytes of globally addressable memory — figures that have not been independently verified.

The system's key innovation is UnifiedBus 2.0, Huawei's proprietary interconnect that functions as a memory fabric: every chip maps the memory of every other chip into its own local address space, making 8,192 processors behave less like a cluster and more like a single machine. The show floor displayed 1,024 chips across 16 cabinets — a fraction of the full configuration.

The most significant specification, though, is not a benchmark: it is that nothing inside the system is American. The Atlas 950 SuperPoD is China's most mature demonstration that frontier AI infrastructure can be built without a single U.S.-origin component. Huawei claims 6.7 times the computing power of Nvidia's NVL144, though that comparison involves different system architectures and should be treated with caution.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the system export controls were designed to prevent. If Huawei's performance claims hold up under independent testing, it means that U.S. chip restrictions have pushed China from dependency toward self-sufficiency in the most strategically important class of computing hardware. The question is no longer whether China can build an AI supercomputer without Nvidia — it is how competitive that supercomputer actually is.

sections.heading: Türkiye Angle: Baykar-Leonardo Partnership Targets Manned-Unmanned Teaming
sections.body:
Turkish drone maker Baykar and Italian defense conglomerate Leonardo will announce joint projects at the Farnborough Airshow this week, centered on human-machine teaming between M-346 fighter jet simulators and Bayraktar KIZILELMA unmanned combat aerial vehicles. The partnership integrates Baykar's unmanned platform expertise with Leonardo's avionics and certification capabilities.

The collaboration signals Baykar's continued move beyond the TB2/Kizilelma paradigm into higher-end autonomous systems that require NATO-interoperable certification and European avionics integration. It also marks a significant deepening of the Turkey-Italy defense industrial relationship, extending a pattern of Turkish defense firms partnering with European primes rather than going it alone on export markets.
sections.whyItMatters: Baykar's partnership with Leonardo — a major NATO-aligned defense electronics maker — represents a strategic bet: that the future of combat aviation is not just drones, but drones paired with crewed platforms in integrated systems. For Türkiye, it means access to European certification pathways and high-end avionics; for Leonardo, it means a proven unmanned platform at scale. The joint projects at Farnborough will reveal whether the two sides can deliver more than a press release.

bottomLine: Three developments converged on Monday that highlight the governance gap in AI. DeepMind's Hassabis proposed a FINRA-style international AI watchdog, acknowledging that the current ad hoc approach to frontier model review isn't sustainable — but the proposal faces a political landscape where Washington is gatekeeping model access, Beijing just launched its own 29-nation governance body, and the EU is implementing its AI Act. Meanwhile, the Trump administration's defense supply chain executive order and AMD's Helios launch both point to the same structural shift: the U.S. is moving from voluntary to mandatory decoupling from Chinese inputs in defense, while the compute market is finally getting a credible alternative to Nvidia. Huawei's Atlas 950 SuperPoD at WAIC reminded everyone that export controls have a half-life — China is building its own stack. The day's throughline: sovereignty, whether over AI governance, mineral supply chains, or compute infrastructure, is being asserted through institutions and products, not just policy statements.
sources: Los Angeles Times, CNBC, Military Times, White House fact sheets, The Hacker News, Censys, AIVD/MIVD advisory, WION News, Unite.AI, MediaNama, Caixin Global, Washington Post, Yahoo News/Space.com, Futurum Group, Bank of America Research, RaillyNews, Arena AI, SiliconANGLE, Bloomberg, Reuters, MSN
